Title: EGFR-Targeted Therapy: A Promising Approach to Treating Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer(NSCLC)Background: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is a leading cause of cancer-related death, with a 5-year survival rate of only 16%. Current treatments, including surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation, have limited effectiveness and often result in recurrence.Methods: We conducted a comprehensive review of the literature on EGFR-targeted therapy for NSCLC. Our search included clinical trials, meta-analyses, and systematic reviews published between 2000 and 2020.Results: We identified 23 eligible studies involving 8,119 patients. The studies included randomized controlled trials, phase II/III trials, and phase IV trials. The results showed that EGFR-targeted therapy was effective in improving progression-free survival and overall survival in patients with NSCLC. We also found that combination therapy involving EGFR-targeted therapy and chemotherapy was more effective than chemotherapy alone. Conclusion: Our review demonstrates the potential of EGFR-targeted therapy in the treatment of NS

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a multifactorial autoimmune disease, which causes inflammation of the joints and ultimately leads to bone erosion. The inflammation is caused by the influx of various cytokines that stimulate the proliferation of RA-affected synovial fibroblasts (RASF) and macrophages. Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a major cytokine involved in RA progression. Overexpression of IL-6 has been observed in patients with RA . The binding of IL-6 to IL-6R leads to the activation of the JAK-STAT3 signaling pathway, which is known to be hyperactivated in RASF and macrophages . Hyperactivation of the JAK-STAT3 signaling pathway increases the production of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), which are involved in the destruction of cartilage and bone. acute lateral patellar dislocation is relatively common in younger age group and most likely caused by indirect trauma . about 10% of acute patella dislocations are the result of a direct blow to the medial side . we report a case of irreducible lateral patellar dislocation which is of exceptional interest in that lateral patellar dislocation ( extra - articular ) occurred in comparatively older age group with arthritic knee , there was no direct trauma involved and it was associated with some rotation along long axis , impaction of patella on lateral femoral condyle and was locked underneath osteophytic ridge of lateral femoral condyle , which needed open reduction . this is a very rare injury , first of its kind being reported in australia .

Inherited BRCA1 mutations predispose women to the development of breast and ovarian cancer. In breast cancer, germline mutations in BRCA1 are associated with an increased risk of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). We examined the relationship between BRCA1 mutations and the development of TNBC in a large cohort of women with breast cancer. We identified 401 women with a BRCA1 mutation, and 720 women with TNBC. Using a multivariate model, we found that BRCA1 mutations are significantly associated with TNBC (p < 0.01). This finding highlights the importance of genetic testing for breast cancer patients, especially those with TNBC. BRCA1 mutation carriers may benefit from early detection strategies and risk-reduction options.

Since the outbreak of COVID-19, extensive efforts have been made to develop effective and safe vaccines. The Pfizer-BioNTech (BNT162b2) mRNA vaccine is one of the most promising COVID-19 vaccines. However, the immunogenicity of BNT162b2 is still unclear. In this study, we investigated the immunogenicity of BNT162b2 vaccine in a population with 15-year-old participants. The participants were divided into two groups: the intervention group, which received two doses of BNT162b2 vaccine, and the control group, which did not receive any vaccines or interventions. The antibody response to the SARS-CoV-2 spike glycoprotein (S1 subunit) was evaluated by ELISA and the neutralizing antibody titer was detected by a microneutralization assay. Breast cancer (BC) is a leading cause of cancer death in women worldwide. Based on their expression of the estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR) and HER2, BC is categorized into four subtypes: Luminal A, Luminal B, HER2-enriched and triple-negative (TNBC). The proportion of TNBC is higher in women of African ancestry than European ancestry. BRCA1 is a high penetrance BC susceptibility gene. Germline mutations in BRCA1 are associated with the highest risk of TNBC. The biological basis for the BRCA1-TNBC association is unknown. To address this question, we examined BRCA1-TNBC tumor samples and their corresponding normal samples from a recently published whole genome sequencing study on African Americans. Compared to the normal samples, we found more loss-of-function BRCA1 mutations in the TNBC tumor samples.
